title: Dyeing with osage orange
subtitle: Simmering Maclura pomifera wood chips to dye alum-mordanted wool a clear yellow, with an iron after-bath for olive
excerpt: Osage orange wood chips give alum-mordanted wool one of the clearest yellows available from a natural dye source. On iron after-bath the yellow shifts to a muted olive-green. The chips require 2 to 4 hours of simmering to fully extract the dye, and the bath is strong enough to dye 2 to 3 successive fibre loads. This tutorial covers chip extraction, the alum dye bath, the optional iron after-bath, and wastewater disposal for the iron modifier.
sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Osage orange colour range by mordant; chip extraction method and reuse.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt or mineral compound that bonds to both the fibre and the dye molecule, creating a stable bridge between them. Alum potash gives the clearest yellow result with osage orange. Iron after-bath darkens and greens the colour considerably.

A separate modifier bath applied after the main dye bath is complete. A dissolved iron or copper salt is added to a small water bath and the dyed fibre is moved into it briefly. The metal ion reacts with the dye already bonded to the fibre, shifting the colour.

A dye extract made by simmering plant material in water, straining out the solids, and using the remaining liquid as the dye bath. Osage orange chips require 2 to 4 hours of simmering to release the full dye content.

Abbreviated as WOF. The dry weight of the fibre being dyed, used as a baseline for calculating how much dye material and mordant to use. A recipe at 50% WOF chips means 50 g of chips per 100 g of dry fibre.

A dye bath from which most of the active dye has been taken up by one fibre load. An exhausted bath still contains enough pigment to dye one or two further loads at successively lighter shades. Osage orange baths exhaust slowly and are useful for 2 to 3 fibre loads.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Wear gloves, an apron, and closed-toe shoes. Work outdoors or with extraction running.

content.text: Osage orange (Maclura pomifera) heartwood chips give alum-
mordanted
wool one of the clearest, most stable yellows available from a plant dye source. The yellow is warm and slightly golden, without the greenish cast that many plant yellows carry. At 50%
weight of fibre
(WOF), the chips give a strong result. An optional iron
after-bath
shifts the result to a muted olive-green.
The chips take longer to extract than most plant dye materials: the
decoction

requires 2 to 4 hours of simmering at 80°C. In return, the spent chips still hold enough pigment to dye 2 to 3 successive loads at lighter shades. The first load gives the clearest yellow; each subsequent load from the same chips produces a progressively paler result from the same
exhausted bath
. Because the after-bath uses iron sulphate, the wastewater section addresses foul-drain disposal for the iron bath.
What you need

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, dry weight
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine-mesh sieve or muslin for straining
Mordant storage jar for alum solution

content.text: Method
Step 1: Optional chip pre-soak

An overnight pre-soak reduces the extraction time needed the following day. Place 50 g of chips in a kilner jar, cover with cold water, and leave overnight. Transfer the chips and soaking liquid to the dye-pot the next morning. If starting without a pre-soak, place the dry chips directly in the pot with water and allow for the longer end of the 2-to-4-hour extraction.
Step 2: Pre-wet the fibre
Submerge the wool in cool water and leave for at least 30 minutes. Pre-wetting ensures even mordant absorption. Squeeze gently without wringing before moving the wool to the mordant bath.
Step 3: Mordant bath

Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water in the mordant jar, then stir into a separate pot of warm water large enough for the wool to move freely. Lower the pre-wetted wool in. Bring to 80°C over 30 minutes and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes. Turn the wool every 10 minutes. Remove the wool and set aside without rinsing. The mordant bath can be stored for one further use once cooled.
Step 4: Extract the dye bath

Transfer the chips (and their soaking liquid if pre-soaked) to the dye-pot. Top up with enough water to allow the wool to move freely once added. Bring to 80°C and hold at 80°C for 2 hours minimum, pressing the chips occasionally with a stirring spoon. Check the colour of the bath: a strong golden-yellow indicates the pigment has extracted well. If the bath looks pale after 2 hours, continue simmering for up to 4 hours total. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing the chips firmly. Reserve the spent chips; they can be re-simmered for 2 further loads. Return the golden-yellow liquid to the pot.
Step 5: Dye bath

Lower the mordanted wool into the strained dye bath. Bring to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold at 80°C for 60 minutes. Turn the wool every 15 minutes. After 45 minutes, lift a small section and rinse quickly under cool water to check the colour: a clear warm yellow is the expected result on alum-mordanted wool. Remove the wool once the colour is where you want it.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

Lift the wool from the dye bath. Cool slightly before rinsing in water at a similar temperature. Rinse until the water runs clear; osage orange typically rinses cleanly with no prolonged bleeding. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade away from direct sun. The yellow on alum-mordanted wool is one of the more light-fast natural dye results, though all plant yellows will lighten gradually over years. If a plain alum result is wanted, the wool is finished here.
Step 7: Iron after-bath (optional)

To shift the clear yellow to a muted olive-green, prepare a separate after-bath: dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of hot water, stir into a pot of warm water at roughly 60°C, and lower the dyed rinsed wool in. Hold at 60°C for 10 to 15 minutes, turning once. The shift from yellow toward olive is pronounced with osage orange: the result is a warm, slightly brownish olive rather than a grey-olive. Remove the wool as soon as the colour is where you want it. Rinse in water at the same temperature until clear, and dry in shade.

attrs.title: Iron mordant disposal
attrs.body: Spent iron bath: foul drain only. Never a storm drain, watercourse, soak-away, or compost. Iron sulphate harms aquatic invertebrates.

content.text: Reusing the chip bath

The spent chips after the first extraction still hold significant dye. For a second load, return the chips to the pot, add fresh water, and re-simmer at 80°C for 60 to 90 minutes; the second extraction gives a softer yellow on alum-mordanted wool, noticeably lighter than the first. A third load from the same chips will give a pale cream-yellow, suitable for subtle accent skeins. After three extractions the chips are fully spent and can go into general waste.
Variations

Alum mordant (25% WOF) with osage orange chips at 50% WOF gives the base clear warm yellow on wool. Increasing the chips to 75% WOF deepens the result toward a stronger, more saturated yellow without shifting the colour family. Reducing to 25% WOF gives a pale gold-yellow.

An iron after-bath (2% WOF iron sulphate) shifts the alum yellow to a muted olive-green. On unmordanted wool the chips give a weak yellow-tan; the alum mordant is what gives osage orange its characteristic clarity. Cotton and linen take a softer, more beige result from the same bath with alum mordant.
Common mistakes

items.problem: Yellow came out dull or muddy rather than clear
items.cause: Extraction was incomplete, or hard water in the dye bath is suppressing the colour
items.fix: Hold the chip extraction at 80°C for the full 2 to 4 hours. Use rainwater or softened water if the tap water in your area is very hard. Hard water can shift yellow dyes toward duller, more greenish results.

items.problem: Colour is pale even with the full chip weight
items.cause: Mordant bath did not reach temperature or was held for insufficient time
items.fix: Ensure the mordant bath reaches 80°C and holds for 45 minutes. The alum bond on wool must be set properly for osage orange to give a clear result.

items.problem: Wool has a slight greenish cast without the iron after-bath
items.cause: Water pH is elevated or water is hard
items.fix: Add a pinch of cream of tartar (5% WOF) to the mordant bath to acidify slightly. This sharpens the yellow and reduces unwanted green shift.

items.cause: Wool not pre-wetted, or too much fibre for the pot volume
items.fix: Pre-wet for at least 30 minutes. Use a pot large enough for the wool to move freely throughout the dye bath without being compressed.

content.text: Wastewater disposal

A plain alum and osage orange dye bath is low-hazard at home quantities. Once cooled, the spent alum bath and the exhausted chip bath can both go down a foul drain. The spent chips go into general waste.

If an iron after-bath was used, that bath must go to a foul drain only. Do not pour iron sulphate solution down a storm drain, into any watercourse, onto a garden soak-away, or into a compost heap. Iron sulphate is harmful to aquatic invertebrates even in small concentrations. For quantities over approximately 2 litres of spent iron bath, contact your local council waste service before disposal.
